title: How to Fix a Wobbling or Noisy Ceiling Fan
question: My ceiling fan wobbles and makes noise — how do I stop it?
summary: Most ceiling-fan wobble comes from loose screws, dust buildup, or slightly unbalanced blades — not a dangerous defect. Tightening the mounting and blade screws, cleaning the blades, and using a clip-on balance kit fixes the large majority. Plan on 20-45 minutes and $0-15.

commonCauses.cause: Loose mounting or blade screws — the most common cause; normal running vibration slowly backs screws out over months
commonCauses.quickCheck: With the fan OFF, gently try to wiggle each blade where it meets the motor, and the round canopy at the ceiling — any play means screws need tightening.

commonCauses.cause: Dust buildup or a slightly bent/sagging blade — uneven weight throws the spin off balance
commonCauses.quickCheck: Hold a yardstick straight up from the floor to one blade tip, spin the blades by hand, and measure each tip to the same point — if they differ by more than about 1/8 inch, a blade is bent or sagging.

commonCauses.cause: Fan hung from a standard light box instead of a fan-rated box, or a worn motor bearing (a grinding or growing hum)
commonCauses.quickCheck: A ceiling fan must hang from a UL-listed fan-rated box, not a normal light box. A grinding noise or a hum that tightening doesn't fix points to the motor bearing, not balance.

fixes: Turn the fan off and let it stop completely. For anything beyond surface cleaning, also turn off the wall switch; if you'll open the canopy to reach the mounting bracket or wiring, turn off the breaker too and confirm power is off with a non-contact voltage tester.

Tighten the blade screws. Each blade attaches to a metal bracket (blade iron) with two or three screws, and the bracket attaches to the motor with more screws. Snug every one with a screwdriver — these are the single most common cause of wobble.

Tighten the mounting at the ceiling. Slide the decorative canopy down to expose the mounting bracket and check that the bracket screws into the box and the downrod set-screws are tight. A loose bracket lets the whole fan rock.
Clean the blades. Wipe both sides of every blade with a slightly damp cloth — caked dust adds uneven weight. Don't press hard enough to bend a blade.
Check for a warped or unequal blade using the yardstick measurement above. If one blade hangs lower, gently bend its bracket to match, or swap a visibly warped blade.

Balance with a clip-on kit ($5-10) if wobble remains. Clip the provided weight to the middle of one blade, run the fan, and move the clip blade-to-blade until you find the worst one; then stick the adhesive weight on that blade where the clip reduced wobble most.

If the box itself moves or isn't fan-rated, stop — replacing a light box with a fan-rated brace box means working in the ceiling and is best done by an electrician. A grinding motor bearing usually means replacing the fan.
diyVsPro: Tightening, cleaning, and balancing are squarely DIY. Call a licensed electrician if the fan is mounted to a standard (non-fan-rated) electrical box, if the box or bracket moves in the ceiling, if you find scorched or melted wiring at the canopy, or if there's aluminum wiring. A persistent grinding motor or a hum that balancing can't cure usually means the fan is worn out and should be replaced rather than repaired.
typicalCostUSD: DIY: $0 to tighten/clean, $5-10 for a clip-on balance kit, ~$15-25 if you also buy a non-contact voltage tester. Pro: roughly $100-200 to replace a non-fan-rated box or rehang a fan, including a typical service minimum; a replacement fan runs $60-300 plus install.
timeEstimate: 20-45 minutes for tightening, cleaning, and balancing.
safetyNote: A spinning fan and ladder work are the real hazards here. Turn the fan fully off before touching the blades, and use a stable ladder — don't overreach. If you open the canopy to reach the bracket or wiring, this becomes 120V electrical work: turn off the breaker and confirm power is off with a non-contact voltage tester (verified first on a known-live outlet) before touching any wire. A fan hung from a box that isn't fan-rated can eventually fall — treat that as a stop-and-call-a-pro finding, not a DIY tighten.
toolsParts: Soft cloth (slightly damp) for the blades
Yardstick or tape measure (to check blade height)
Non-contact voltage tester (only if opening the canopy/wiring)
searchTerms: how to fix a wobbly ceiling fan
sources: Reputable DIY references (This Old House, Family Handyman, Bob Vila)
Ceiling-fan manufacturer installation and balancing instructions (e.g. Hunter, Hampton Bay)
UL listing norms for fan-rated electrical boxes
disclaimer: This guide is general home-maintenance information, not professional electrical advice. If the fan is improperly mounted or you find damaged wiring, stop and consult a licensed electrician.
verifierNote: Confirmed that loose hardware is the dominant cause, that a fan-rated box is a code/safety requirement (non-fan-rated box = stop-and-call-a-pro, not a DIY tighten), and that balance-kit and replacement costs reflect current US ranges. Kept ladder + power-off-and-verify as firm safety gates.
